docs.text: terminology (May 27, 2021) D: the lower mantle starting at 1000-km depth D': mesosphere (between 1000-km and D") D": the lowermost mantle MORB: midocean-ridge basalts Ma: million years PKJKP: seismic wave that traverses the IC as a shear wave P'P': wave that goes through the core and reflects off of the opposite side of the earth Preface and Philosophy (May 27, 2021) object: a better understanding of origin, structure, composition and evolution of our planet multidisciplinarity is more essential than ever seismologist: apt to interpret velocity anomalies in terms of temperature variations in a homogeneous, isotropic model petrologists: variations in mineralogy, crystal orientation or partial melt content counts more than temperature in the seismic velocity isotope geochemists: 650-km discontinuity, tend to interpret the chemical data in terms of ancient isolated reservoirs; exotic basalts are assumed to be from the CMB; MORB are viewed as a unique component of the mantle, rather than as an average composition of a heterogeneous population. convection theoretician: treats the mantle as a homogeneous fluid or as a 2-layered system, driven by temperature-induced buoyancy. Bias: rocks and magmas only represent shallow mantle and crust; seismic data is controlled by many factors, not only temperature-density current Earth paradigms from different disciplines: seismologists prefer a whole-mantle convection, while geochemists prefer layered convection deep-mantle features may be convectively isolated from upper-mantle processes. part 2: a dynamic planet (May 27, 2021) Plates are separated by boundaries and meet at triple junctions. In view of thermodynamics, the plates are driven by the thermal and density variations in the mantle . Cooling plates and sinking slabs can also be regarded as driving themselves, and driving convection in the underlying mantle. The cold stiff outer shell of earth is the active element and the template; the underlying convective mantle is passive. Ridge-trench-slab system is the main plate tectonic cycle. chap 4: the outer shell of the earth (June 1, 2021) Definition of plates is partly differnent from that of the lithosphere. It breaks at suture zones, fracture zones, subplate boundaries.

docs.text: object: a better understanding of origin, structure, composition and evolution of our planet multidisciplinarity is more essential than ever seismologist: apt to interpret velocity anomalies in terms of temperature variations in a homogeneous, isotropic model petrologists: variations in mineralogy, crystal orientation or partial melt content counts more than temperature in the seismic velocity isotope geochemists: 650-km discontinuity, tend to interpret the chemical data in terms of ancient isolated reservoirs; exotic basalts are assumed to be from the CMB; MORB are viewed as a unique component of the mantle, rather than as an average composition of a heterogeneous population. convection theoretician: treats the mantle as a homogeneous fluid or as a 2-layered system, driven by temperature-induced buoyancy. Bias: rocks and magmas only represent shallow mantle and crust; seismic data is controlled by many factors, not only temperature-density current Earth paradigms from different disciplines: seismologists prefer a whole-mantle convection, while geochemists prefer layered convection deep-mantle features may be convectively isolated from upper-mantle processes.

docs.text: Plates are separated by boundaries and meet at triple junctions. In view of thermodynamics, the plates are driven by the thermal and density variations in the mantle . Cooling plates and sinking slabs can also be regarded as driving themselves, and driving convection in the underlying mantle. The cold stiff outer shell of earth is the active element and the template; the underlying convective mantle is passive. Ridge-trench-slab system is the main plate tectonic cycle.

docs.text: Geodynamics in 2021 spring, lectured by Prof. Leng definition Geodynamics is a subfield of geophysics dealing with dynamics of the Earth. It applies physics, chemistry and mathematics to the understanding of how mantle convection leads to plate tectonics and geologic phenomena such as seafloor spreading, mountain building, volcanoes, earthquakes, faulting and so on. overview Geodynamics is generally concerned with processes that move materials throughout the Earth. In the Earth's interior, movement happens when rocks melt or deform and flow in response to a stress field. deformation of rocks elastic: < yield strength & << relaxation time The magnitude of the stress cannot exceed the yield strength of a material, and the time scale of the stress cannot approach the relaxation time of the material . If stress exceeds the yield strength of a material, bonds begin to break, which can lead to ductile or brittle deformation. ductile: > yield strength & = relaxation time Ductile deformation happens when the temperature of a system is high enough so that a significant fraction of the material microstates are unbound. Examples include bending of the lithosphere under volcanic islands or sedimentary basins, and bending at oceanic trenches. brittle: > yield strength & < relaxation time When strain localizes faster than these relaxation processes can redistribute it , brittle deformation occurs. thermodynamics The most important thermodynamic variables are temperature and pressure. Within the upper lithosphere, brittle deformation is common. After the brittle-ductile transition zone, ductile deformation becomes dominant. Forces The main motive force behind stress in the Earth is provided by the energy from radioisotope decay, friction, and residual heat . Negative thermal buoyancy of the oceanic plates is the primary cause of subduction and plate tectonics, while positive thermal buoyancy may lead to mantle plumes, which could explain intraplate volcanism . Methods Data includes field observations, geodesy, radiometric dating, petrology, mineralogy, drilling boreholes and remote sensing techniques . Numerical modeling Forward modeling to: reproduce a specific observation produce basic fluid dynamics Basic fluid dynamics modelling can further be subdivided into instantaneous studies , which aim to reproduce the instantaneous flow in a system due to a given buoyancy distribution, and time-dependent studies , which either aim to reproduce a possible evolution of a given initial condition over time or a statistical (quasi) steady-state of a given system. Basic SI unit & constant density rayleigh number A picture of Geodynamics (Layer cake or plum pudding ?)

docs.text: Plate Tectonics The World Atlas names seven major plates: African Antarctic Eurasian Indo-Australian North American Pacific South American. How to define a plate vertically & horizontally? Tectonic plates are gigantic pieces of the Earth's crust and uppermost mantle, i.e. pieces of the lithosphere . (vertically) The lithosphere, which is the rigid outermost shell of a planet (the crust and upper mantle), is broken into tectonic plates. moving as a whole (horizontally) The types of plate boundaries convergent divergent transform how to calculate plate motion ? current motion: GPS & InSAR historical motion: motion of paleo-magnetic dipole what is Wilson Cycle ? The Wilson Cycle is a model that describes the opening and closing of ocean basins and the subduction and divergence of tectonic plates during the assembly and disassembly of supercontinents. Phases of Wilson cycle: 1. From ten o'clock position clockwise: (10) initial pre-drift extension, 2. (12) rift-to-drift phase, initial opening of an oceanic basin, 3. (2 and 4) seafloor spreading, widening of the basin, 4. (6) subduction of oceanic lithosphere, closure of the basin, 5. (8) continent-continent collision evidence for plume hypotheisis: mantle plume hypotheisis is a nice theory to explain the observed hotspot and tracks. A supplementary to plate tetonics theory. the characteristics of hotspot: 1. high heat flow (hotspots) 2. high swell topography 3. hotspot track supercontinents Pangea decomposes to Laurasia & Gondwana

In order to know the heat flux from the Earth, rock's thermal conductivity \(k\) & temperature gradient \(\nabla T\) near surface are needed Earth's heat budget: \(Q_{mantle}=Q_{cmb}+Q_{sec}+Q_{rad}\) 1. internal heating ratio = \(1 - \frac{Q_{cmb}}{Q_{mantle}}\) 2. heat generation by the decay of radioactive elements (U, Tl, K) Half space cooling model Equation: \(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}=\kappa \frac{\partial^2T}{\partial y^2}\) boundary condtitions: $T=T_1, t=0, y>0 \ T=T_0, y=0, t>0 \ T\to T_1, y\to \infty, t>0 $ solution: \(\frac{T-T_1}{T_0-T_1}=erfc(\frac{y}{2\sqrt{\kappa t}})\) typically, we set \(t=x/\mu\) , where \(x\) is the distance between the point to the mid-ocean ridge plate cooling model Equation: \(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}=\kappa \frac{\partial^2T}{\partial y^2}\) define \(y_{L_0}\) , the plate thickness boundary condtitions: $T=T_1, t=0, y_{L_0}\geqslant y\geqslant 0 \ T=T_0, y=0, t>0 \ T=T_1, y=y_{y_{L_0}}, t>0 \ $

docs.text: Gravity Surface observables curvature, heat, gravity and geoid, ... Terminology: gravity anomaly: the difference between the observed acceleration of an object in free fall on a planet's surface, and the corresponding value predicted from a model of the planet's gravitational field . (Caused by the topography and density inhomogeneities . Notice that isostasy remove the anomaly of large-scale topography ) gravity equipotential: a surface on which the gravity potential \(U\) is a constant geoid: the shape of that the ocean surface would take under the influence of the gravity and rotation of Earth alone , if other influences such as winds and tides are absent. This surface is extended through the continents . It is a smooth but irregular surface whose shape results from the uneven distribution of mass within and on the surface of Earth . The surface of the geoid is higher than the reference ellipsoid wherever there is a positive gravity anomaly and lower than the reference ellipsoid wherever there is a negative gravity anomaly . reference spheroid: typically defined as a spheroid geoid anomaly: \(U - U_{ref}\) isostatic geoid anomaly: it reflects the dipole moment of the density distribution gravity anomalies in some typical tectonic settings positive (mine); negative (petroleum); unconceivable (mantle convection involves both less dense material due to hotter lava & topographic bulge ) Equation: \(g_m = \frac{GM}{r^2}\) geoid surface vs. reference ellipsoid vs. physical Earth geoid reference physical irregular smooth most irregular gravity anomaly category surface Bouguer density anomalies topographic workflow of gravity data processing get the data by CG-5 remove the reference gravity field elevation (free-air) correction, you will get the free-air gravity anomaly topography correction, after that you will get the Bouguer anomaly (Bouguer & free-air anomaly are the most frequently used gravity data) Compensation model: Airy compensation (by depleted mantle) \(Th_{root}(\rho_{mantle}-\rho_{crust})=Th_{range}(\rho_{crust}-\rho_{air})\\ Th_{anti-root}(\rho_{mantle}-\rho_{crust})=Th_{water}(\rho_{crust}-\rho_{water})\) Pratt compensation (by varied density) \(\rho_0Th_0=\rho_-(Th_0+Th_{range})\\ \rho_0Th_0=\rho_+(Th_0-Th_{water})+\rho_{water}Th_{water}\) origin of the long-wavelength geoid anomaly mantle convection origin of the short-wavelength gravity anomaly mantle convection

docs.text: Faults and Rock Rheology Elastic, viscous and plastic behavior in the Earth's interior Terminology Rheology: the study of the flow of the matter, primarily in a liquid or gas state, but also as "soft solids" or solids under conditions in which they respond with plastic flow rather than deforming elastically in response to applied force. Newtonian fluid: a fluid in which the viscous stresses arising from its flow, at every point, are linearly correlated to the local strain rate --the rate of change of its deformation over time. That is equivalent to saying those forces are proportional to the rates of change of the fluids's velocity vector as one moves away from the point in question in various directions. dextral: on the right side or the right hand. For sinistral, vice versa. Rock failure and faults rocks are brittle at atmosphere pressure and room temperature elastic behavior until faults forms faults are introduced when the shear stress is larger than the yield stress. The yield stress increases with the normal stress (Byerlee's law) . Fault types Normal faults: \(\sigma_{yy}=\rho gy, \sigma_{xx}=\rho gy +\Delta \sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{zz}=\rho gy +\nu \Delta \sigma_{xx}\) (due to \(\epsilon_{zz}=0\) ), where \(\sigma_{xx}\lt0\) Thrust faults: the same as Normal fault, but \(\sigma_{xx}>0\) strike-slip faults: Byerlee's law It concerns the shear stress required to slide one rock over another . The rocks have macroscopically flat surfaces, but the surfaces have small asperities that make them "rough". For a given experiment and at normal stresses \(\sigma_n\) below about 2000 bars (200 Mpa) the shear stress increases approximately linearly with the normal stress ( \(\tau=0.85\sigma_n\) ) and is highly dependent on rock type and the character (roughness) of the surfaces. Byerlee's law states that with increased normal stress the required shear stress continues to increase, but the rate of increase decreases ( \(\tau=0.5+0.6\sigma_n\) ) , and becomes nearly independent of rock type . Mohr-coulomb yielding criterion \(\tau=\sigma \tan (\phi)+c\) \(\phi\) is called the angle of internal friction, it's a intrinsic property of the rocks \(c\) is cohesion For wet rock with a pore pressure (neglecting cohesion), \(|\tau|=f_s(\sigma_n-p_w)\) In the context of hydraulic fracturing in shale gas mining, defining an effective coefficient of friction . Anderson's theory of faulting Anderson recognized that since the principal stress directions are directions of zero shear stress, we can place faults in the context of principal stress. All faults have a common function, to extend the crust in one direction and shorten it in another. The directions of shortening and stretching are at right angles to one another. For a thrust faulting (assumed with dip angle \(\beta\) ), we get \(\Delta \sigma_{xx}\propto \frac{1}{\pm \sin(2\theta)-f_s(1+cos(2\theta))}\) , where \(\theta=\pi/2-\beta\) . Faulting will occur at angles that minimize \(|\Delta \sigma_{xx}|\) , so \(\tan(2\beta) = \pm \frac{1}{f_s}\) & \(\Delta \sigma_{xx}=\frac{\pm 2f_s(\rho gy-p_w)}{(1+f_s^2)^{1/2}\mp f_s}\) Evidence of the viscous layering in the Earth's mantle long-wavelength geoid anomalies mantle convection wavelength inversion from post-glacial rebound: good for two-layer model, trade-off for four-layer model

docs.text: Rheology: the study of the flow of the matter, primarily in a liquid or gas state, but also as "soft solids" or solids under conditions in which they respond with plastic flow rather than deforming elastically in response to applied force. Newtonian fluid: a fluid in which the viscous stresses arising from its flow, at every point, are linearly correlated to the local strain rate --the rate of change of its deformation over time. That is equivalent to saying those forces are proportional to the rates of change of the fluids's velocity vector as one moves away from the point in question in various directions. dextral: on the right side or the right hand. For sinistral, vice versa.

docs.text: It concerns the shear stress required to slide one rock over another . The rocks have macroscopically flat surfaces, but the surfaces have small asperities that make them "rough". For a given experiment and at normal stresses \(\sigma_n\) below about 2000 bars (200 Mpa) the shear stress increases approximately linearly with the normal stress ( \(\tau=0.85\sigma_n\) ) and is highly dependent on rock type and the character (roughness) of the surfaces. Byerlee's law states that with increased normal stress the required shear stress continues to increase, but the rate of increase decreases ( \(\tau=0.5+0.6\sigma_n\) ) , and becomes nearly independent of rock type .

docs.text: Anderson recognized that since the principal stress directions are directions of zero shear stress, we can place faults in the context of principal stress. All faults have a common function, to extend the crust in one direction and shorten it in another. The directions of shortening and stretching are at right angles to one another. For a thrust faulting (assumed with dip angle \(\beta\) ), we get \(\Delta \sigma_{xx}\propto \frac{1}{\pm \sin(2\theta)-f_s(1+cos(2\theta))}\) , where \(\theta=\pi/2-\beta\) . Faulting will occur at angles that minimize \(|\Delta \sigma_{xx}|\) , so \(\tan(2\beta) = \pm \frac{1}{f_s}\) & \(\Delta \sigma_{xx}=\frac{\pm 2f_s(\rho gy-p_w)}{(1+f_s^2)^{1/2}\mp f_s}\)

docs.text: Geodynamics in 2021 spring, lectured by Prof. Leng terminology Geodynamics: a subfield of geophysics dealing with dynamics of the Earth. It applies physics, chemistry and mathematics to the understanding of how mantle convection leads to plate tectonics and geologic phenomena such as seafloor spreading, mountain building, volcanoes, earthquakes, faulting and so on. overview Geodynamics is generally concerned with processes that move materials throughout the Earth. In the Earth's interior, movement happens when rocks melt or deform and flow in response to a stress field. deformation of rocks elastic: < yield strength & << relaxation time The magnitude of the stress cannot exceed the yield strength of a material, and the time scale of the stress cannot approach the relaxation time of the material . If stress exceeds the yield strength of a material, bonds begin to break, which can lead to ductile or brittle deformation. ductile: > yield strength & = relaxation time Ductile deformation happens when the temperature of a system is high enough so that a significant fraction of the material microstates are unbound. Examples include bending of the lithosphere under volcanic islands or sedimentary basins, and bending at oceanic trenches. brittle: > yield strength & < relaxation time When strain localizes faster than these relaxation processes can redistribute it , brittle deformation occurs. Thermodynamics The most important thermodynamic variables are temperature and pressure. Within the upper lithosphere, brittle deformation is common. After the brittle-ductile transition zone, ductile deformation becomes dominant . Forces The main motive force behind stress in the Earth is provided by the energy from radioisotope decay, friction, and residual heat . Negative thermal buoyancy of the oceanic plates is the primary cause of subduction and plate tectonics, while positive thermal buoyancy may lead to mantle plumes, which could explain intraplate volcanism . Methods Data includes field observations, geodesy, radiometric dating, petrology, mineralogy, drilling boreholes and remote sensing techniques . Numerical modeling Forward modeling to: reproduce a specific observation produce basic fluid dynamics Basic fluid dynamics modelling can further be subdivided into instantaneous studies , which aim to reproduce the instantaneous flow in a system due to a given buoyancy distribution, and time-dependent studies , which either aim to reproduce a possible evolution of a given initial condition over time or a statistical (quasi) steady-state of a given system. Basic SI unit & typical constants density: 3000; 2700; 1000 tectonic stress: 10 Mpa (1 pa = 1 N/m2; 1 Mpa = 1e6 pa; 1 Mpa = 10 bar) Young's Modulus for typical rocks: 70 Gpa magnitude for strain in the lithosphere: 1e-4 Possion's ratio: \(\nu=0.25\) heat flux: a flow of energy per unit of area per unit of time. 65 +- 1.6 mWm-2 for continent; 101 +- 2.2 mWm-1 for ocean thermal conductivity k: a measure of its ability to conduct heat. Wm-1K-1 heat capacity: the amount of heat to be supplied to an object to produce a unit change in its temperature. A picture of Geodynamics (Layer cake or plum pudding ?)

docs.text: Interior of the Earth, lectured by Prof. Mao some typical constants spreading rate: vary between ~0.5-10 cm/yr key problems variations of the transition zone: caused by the thermal variations in the mid mantle (the hotter, the deeper 410-km discontinuity is; the colder, the deeper 660-km discontunity is, due to negative Clapeyron slope ) structures of the mesosphere (lower mantle): 660–770 km: A discontinuity in compression wave velocity (6-11%) followed by a steep gradient is indicative of the transformation of the mineral ringwoodite to bridgmanite and ferropericlase and the transition between the transition zone layer to the lower mantle; 770–2700 km: A gradual increase in velocity indicative of the adiabatic compression of the mineral phases in the lower mantle; 1200 km discontunity : a conversion of the high pressure structure of the quartz, the stishovite , into the CaCl2 structure could be one reason. 2700–2900 km: The D'' layer is considered the transition from the lower mantle to the outer core. can a slab penetrate the 660-km discontunity ? difficult, due to composition segregation within the MTZ and chemical filtering what is the evidence of laminar flow within the mantle for geochemists ? global comparison of the 3He/4He ratio between OIB and MORB. the relative uniformaity of MORB values combined with the more variable 3He/4He ratios observed in the OIB has motivated conceptual models of Earth's mantle that involve a relatively degassed, homogenous background mantle source for MORBs and a deeper, primordial (less degassed) reservoir that is trapped by mantle plumes to produce OIBs at the surface . explain two mechanisms for heat flowing through the permeable rock and sediments: conduction through the solids; water flow through pores and fracture because the fresh basalt is very permeable and the heat drives water convection. what is the source of the heat near the surface of the Earth ? much of the heat is created by decay of naturally radioactive elements. gravitational potential energy that can be further divided into: heat released during the accretion of the Earth & heat released during differentiation, as abundant heavy metals descended to the Earth's core. latent heat released as the liquid outer core crystallizes at the inner core boundary tidal forces on the Earth as it rotates . Analyse the feasiblity for two kinds of differentiation mechanisms: transport of liquid metal through solid silicates, or through patially or fully molten silicate magma ocean. the first case is possible, because iron (plus alloying elemets) has a lower melting temperature than mantle silicates, so that liquid iron can coexist with solid silicates ; could be too sluggish (i.e. slow) for the second case, it has to be a significant process during core formation. Explain the formation of the Asthenosphere. melts maybe. partial melt could: lower the seismic velocity of mantle rocks; increase the electrical conductivity. Classification of igneous rocks: plutonic (深成的) rocks & volcanic lavas. Gabbro (辉长岩) & Basalt. intrusive & extrusive. grain size & how quake the cooling is. explain the seismic Moho & petrological Moho. Immediately above the Moho, the velocities of primary seismic waves (P-waves) are consistent with those through basalt (6.7–7.2 km/s), and below they are similar to those through peridotite or dunite (7.6–8.6 km/s). why higher mantle temperature leads to thicker oceanic crust ? to be updated ... terminology pyrolite: A term used to characterize a model composition of the Earth's mantle. ( Ol, Wad, Ring, Fp 铁方镁石, Pv, Gt石榴子石, Opx, Cpx, Ca-Pv ) This model is based on that a pyrolite source can produce the MORB by partial melting. It consists of 1 part basalt and 3 parts dunite (90% olivine) . Ol, Wad, and Ring have the same composition but different structure. Wad and Ring are called high-pressure polymorphs of olivine . CI Chondrites: sometimes called C1 chondrites, are a group of rare stony meteorites belonging to the carbonaceous chondrites炭质球粒陨石 . Compared to all the meteorites found so far, their chemical composition most closely resembles the elemental distribution in the sun's photosphere . The main characteristic of CI chondrites is the lack of recognizable chondrules陨石球粒 . decompression melting: The solidus temperatures of most rocks increase with increasing pressure in the absence of water. Peridotite (橄榄岩) at depth in the Earth's mantle may be hotter than its solidus temperature at some shallower level. If such rock rises during the convection of solid mantle, it will cool slightly as it expands in an adiabatic process. If the rock rises far enough, it will begin to melt. Basalt: a fine-grained extrusive igneous rock formed from the rapid cooling of low-viscosity lava rich in magnesium and iron (i.e. Mg & Fe, mafic lava) exposed at or very near the surface of a rocky planet or a mooon. It has density of 3.0 g/cm3 and 5-7 km thick. And it is geologically young, dark in color. It composed the oceanic crust . OIB: ocean island basalts (i.e. hotspots in the ocean, due to mantle plume ) Granite (花岗岩): a coarse-grained igneous rock composed mostly of quartz, alkali feldspar (钾长石), and plagioclase (斜长石). It forms from magma with a high content of silica and alkali metal oxides that slowly cools and solidifies underground. It is common in the Earth's continental crust, where it is found in various kinds of igneous intrusions. These range in size from dykes only a few inches across to batholiths exposed over hundreds of square kilometers. It has density of 2.7 g/cm3, and 30-50 km thick. It can be very old, light in color, and rich in Na, K, Ca, Al. Amphiboles (角闪石); Pyroxene (辉石); Micas (云母) Equilibrium Geotherms: temperature-depth profile within the Earth is called geotherms . In the context of a state of equilibrium in which the temperature at any points is steady, the profile is called equilibrium geotherms. This is controled by a 2-nd order PDE. Internal heat budget: The flow of heat from Earth's interior to the surface comes from two main sources in roughly equal amounts: the radiogenic heat produced by the radioactive decay of isotopes in the mantle and crust, and the primordial heat left over from the formation of Earth. Adiabatic expansion (or compression): it causes a drop in temperature. See compression melting for comparison.
